title: DAVID — The Court Language Hidden in Plain Sight

brand.title: DAVID · The Archive
brand.subtitle: Dead languages, actually pronounced.
brand.cta: Bring the dead tongues back — one attestation at a time. DAVID.

beats.speech_text: I am DAVID. Today, Classical Japanese — the court language of the Man'yōshū and The Tale of Genji, still hiding inside modern Japanese, unchanged on the surface but profoundly different underneath.

Modern Japanese speakers can read the characters — but the grammar, the verb endings, and the sounds of the Nara and Heian courts have shifted so far that classical texts read like a foreign language if you try to parse them as modern speech.
So I ask the question I ask of every supposedly dead tongue: who kept speaking it, and what does the corpus preserve about its sound?

The proof is in Man'yōgana — Chinese characters used purely for their sound value to write Japanese phonetics — giving us the oldest direct record of Japanese syllables, locked inside the four thousand five hundred poems of the Man'yōshū, compiled around 759 CE.

We reconstruct Classical Japanese corpus-first: Man'yōgana syllable inventories identify contrasts lost in modern Japanese, Heian-period grammars record verb conjugation tables, and Buddhist ritual texts preserve archaic readings standardised by monks for centuries.

The syllable inventory is well-attested; the pitch accent and the eight-vowel system of the Man'yōshū period are partially reconstructed from dialect evidence and comparative Ryukyuan. I label each claim accordingly.
Listen — umi no umi no / kame mo kiku to ya / wata tsu umi no — a fragment from the Man'yōshū, recited as the Nara court would have heard it, with the older vowel distinctions the writing preserves.
beats.on_screen: ATTESTED TEXT · Classical Japanese · Man'yōshū, 759 CE

beats.speech_text: Follow the Archive, and the language they told you was frozen starts moving again — one attestation at a time.
